title: How to spot rip currents in the ocean
description: MOSSEL BAY NEWS - Here's what to look...
bodyText: [{"type":"paragraph","text":"MOSSEL BAY NEWS - Here's what to look out for to avoid rip currents:","html":"MOSSEL BAY NEWS - Here's what to look out for to avoid rip currents:"},{"type":"list","items":["Water through a surf zone that is a different colour to the surrounding water.","A change in the incoming pattern of waves (often the waves are not breaking in a rip channel).","Seaweed, sand 'clouds' or debris moving out to the backline where waves are forming through the surf zone.","Turbulent or choppy water in the surf zone in a channel- or river-like shape flowing away from the beach."],"ordered":false},{"type":"blockquote","text":"The NSRI says a rip current will not pull you under the water; as long as you can float, you will be safe until you can escape the current by swimming to the side (out of it) and then back to the beach."},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Be sure to maintain a slow and relaxed pace until you reach the shore or assistance arrives. If you are swimming at a beach where lifeguards are on duty, raise your arm and wave for help.","html":"Be sure to maintain a slow and relaxed pace until you reach the shore or assistance arrives. If you are swimming at a beach where lifeguards are on duty, raise your arm and wave for help."},{"type":"paragraph","text":"If you see someone in a rip current, do not go in to help unless you are trained and have emergency flotation devices such as the NSRI Pink Rescue Buoy or a surf board.","html":"If you see someone in a rip current, do not go in to help unless you are trained and have emergency flotation devices such as the NSRI Pink Rescue Buoy or a surf board."},{"type":"paragraph","text":"‘We bring you the latest Garden Route, Hessequa, Karoo news’","html":"‘We bring you the latest Garden Route, Hessequa, Karoo news’"}]

images.alt: How to spot rip currents in the ocean
